challenges.title: Print a greeting
challenges.description: Print the text 'Hello, World!' to the console.
challenges.hint: Use System.out.println() with a string argument.

steps.title: Understand the entry point
steps.explanation: Every Java program starts from the main method inside a class. The method signature must be exactly public static void main(String[] args).

steps.title: Print to the console
steps.explanation: System.out.println() writes a line to standard output and adds a newline at the end.

steps.title: Verify
steps.explanation: Compile and run. You should see 'Hello, World!' in the console.

challenges.title: Check palindrome
challenges.description: Write a method `isPalindrome(String s)` that returns true if the string reads the same forwards and backwards (case-insensitive), false otherwise.
challenges.hint: Reverse the string and compare it to the original. Remember to handle case.

steps.title: Normalize case
steps.explanation: Convert the string to lower case so the comparison is case-insensitive.

steps.title: Reverse the string
steps.explanation: StringBuilder.reverse() efficiently reverses the character sequence.

steps.title: Compare
steps.explanation: Use .equals() (not ==) to compare string contents in Java.

challenges.title: Two Sum
challenges.description: Given an integer array `nums` and an integer `target`, return the indices of the two numbers that add up to `target`. Each input has exactly one solution. You may not use the same element twice.
challenges.hint: A HashMap lets you check in O(1) whether the complement of each number has already been seen.

steps.title: Use a HashMap
steps.explanation: Store each number and its index as you iterate. For each element, check whether its complement (target - nums[i]) is already in the map.

steps.title: Single pass
steps.explanation: This approach is O(n) time and O(n) space — a single loop through the array.

challenges.title: Thread-safe singleton
challenges.description: Implement a thread-safe Singleton class using the double-checked locking pattern. The class must expose a `getInstance()` method and an `increment()` / `getCount()` pair for a shared counter.
challenges.hint: Declare the instance field as volatile and use synchronized on the inner null check.

steps.title: volatile field
steps.explanation: volatile ensures that writes to the instance field are immediately visible to all threads, preventing stale reads.

steps.title: Double-checked locking
steps.explanation: Check instance == null before and after the synchronized block. The outer check avoids locking on every call; the inner check prevents duplicate creation.

steps.title: Synchronized counter methods
steps.explanation: Mark increment() and getCount() as synchronized to prevent data races on the count field.
steps.code:
public synchronized void increment() { count++; }
public synchronized int getCount() { return count; }
